content.text: Microsoft Learn (learn.microsoft.com) is Microsoft's free, structured learning platform for Azure and all Microsoft products. It provides self-paced modules with interactive exercises, free sandbox Azure environments, guided learning paths, and the official study materials for Azure certification exams. No credit card or Azure subscription is needed to complete most exercises.

attrs.alt: Microsoft Learn content hierarchy: Learning Paths → Modules → Units, with free Azure Sandbox environments

content.text: Reading + embedded exercise or knowledge check
Learning a single concept or task
Mastering a specific topic (e.g. AKS networking)
Ordered sequence of modules toward a goal
Preparing for a certification or role transition
Hands-on without managing your own subscription
Demonstrating job-ready skills with a credential
Mapped to exam objectives with practice assessments
AZ-900, AZ-104, AZ-204, AZ-305, and others

Many modules include a free Azure sandbox — a temporary subscription provisioned specifically for that exercise. The sandbox contains pre-created resource groups and, in some modules, pre-deployed resources. It is deleted automatically after the session, and using it consumes no credits from your own subscription.

Sandbox environments are independent of your Azure account. You sign in with your Microsoft Learn account (a personal Microsoft account), and the resources are provisioned under Microsoft's training tenant. No billing occurs on your subscription.
A real Azure subscription under a Microsoft-controlled training tenant.
Pre-created resource groups (e.g. 'learn-rg-a1b2c3') for the exercise.
Access to Azure CLI via the integrated Cloud Shell — no local install required.
Quota for the specific services covered by the module (not full subscription quota).
Automatic teardown after the module session ends (typically 1–4 hours).
Running CLI Commands in the Integrated Cloud Shell

# The Learn sandbox includes a Cloud Shell powered by Azure Cloud Shell
# Commands run against the sandbox subscription automatically

# Verify the sandbox subscription and resource group
az account show --output table
az group list --output table

# Most exercises follow a pattern: create resource → configure → verify
# Example from the AKS module:
az aks create \
  --resource-group learn-rg-abc123 \
  --name my-aks \
  --node-count 1 \
  --generate-ssh-keys \
  --enable-managed-identity

# Get credentials
az aks get-credentials --resource-group learn-rg-abc123 --name my-aks

# Deploy a workload
kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Azure-Samples/aks-store-demo/main/aks-store-quickstart.yaml

# Verify
kubectl get services --namespace aks-store-demo
Azure certifications are structured in three tiers: Fundamentals, Associate, and Expert. Microsoft Learn provides official study materials mapped directly to each exam's skill measurement document.
Navigating to Exam Study Materials Programmatically

import requests

def fetch_learn_catalog(
    type_filter: str = "learningPath",  # learningPath | module | unit
    product_filter: str = "azure",
    locale: str = "en-us",
) -> list[dict]:
    """
    Query the Microsoft Learn catalog API to discover learning paths for Azure.
    Returns a list of content items matching the filters.
    """
    url = "https://learn.microsoft.com/api/catalog/"
    params = {
        "locale": locale,
        "type": type_filter,
        "products": product_filter,
    }
    resp = requests.get(url, params=params, timeout=30)
    resp.raise_for_status()
    items = resp.json().get("learningPaths" if type_filter == "learningPath" else "modules", [])

    return [
        {
            "uid": item["uid"],
            "title": item["title"],
            "duration_minutes": item.get("duration_in_minutes", 0),
            "levels": item.get("levels", []),
            "roles": item.get("roles", []),
            "url": f"https://learn.microsoft.com/training/paths/{item['uid'].split('.')[-1]}",
        }
        for item in items
    ]

# Find beginner Azure learning paths
paths = fetch_learn_catalog("learningPath", "azure")
beginner = [p for p in paths if "beginner" in p.get("levels", [])]
print(f"Beginner Azure learning paths: {len(beginner)}")
for path in sorted(beginner, key=lambda x: -x["duration_minutes"])[:5]:
    hrs = path["duration_minutes"] // 60
    mins = path["duration_minutes"] % 60
    print(f"  [{hrs}h {mins}m] {path['title']}")
    print(f"     {path['url']}")

Microsoft Learn uses an experience point (XP) system to track progress. Completing units, modules, and learning paths earns XP and trophies displayed on your public Learn profile. This is separate from certification — XP reflects learning activity, not exam results.
Querying Your Learn Profile via the API

import requests

def get_learn_profile(username: str) -> dict:
    """
    Fetch public Microsoft Learn profile data for a given username.
    username: the Learn profile slug (visible in your profile URL)
    """
    url = f"https://learn.microsoft.com/api/profiles/{username}"
    resp = requests.get(url, timeout=15)
    if resp.status_code == 404:
        return {"error": f"Profile '{username}' not found"}
    resp.raise_for_status()
    data = resp.json()

    return {
        "display_name": data.get("displayName"),
        "xp": data.get("xp", 0),
        "level": data.get("level", 1),
        "completed_modules": len(data.get("completedModules", [])),
        "completed_paths": len(data.get("completedLearningPaths", [])),
        "certifications": [
            c.get("examCode") for c in data.get("certifications", [])
        ],
        "achievements": [
            a.get("title") for a in data.get("achievements", [])
        ][:5],
    }

profile = get_learn_profile("your-learn-username")
print(f"Name: {profile['display_name']}")
print(f"XP: {profile['xp']:,} (Level {profile['level']})")
print(f"Modules completed: {profile['completed_modules']}")
print(f"Certifications: {', '.join(profile['certifications']) or 'None yet'}")
Integrating Microsoft Learn into Team Onboarding

Teams can assign specific learning paths to new hires and track completion as part of onboarding. The Learn catalog API makes it straightforward to build a simple onboarding tracker that maps roles to required learning paths.

import json
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import List

@dataclass
class OnboardingPlan:
    role: str
    required_paths: List[str]     # Learn path UIDs
    recommended_certs: List[str]  # Exam codes (e.g. "AZ-104")
    target_weeks: int

ONBOARDING_PLANS = {
    "cloud-engineer": OnboardingPlan(
        role="Cloud Engineer",
        required_paths=[
            "learn.azure.azure-fundamentals",       # AZ-900 prep
            "learn.azure.administrator-associate",  # AZ-104 prep
        ],
        recommended_certs=["AZ-900", "AZ-104"],
        target_weeks=8,
    ),
    "platform-engineer": OnboardingPlan(
        role="Platform Engineer",
        required_paths=[
            "learn.azure.azure-fundamentals",
            "learn.azure.devops-engineer-expert",   # AZ-400 prep
        ],
        recommended_certs=["AZ-900", "AZ-400"],
        target_weeks=10,
    ),
    "data-engineer": OnboardingPlan(
        role="Data Engineer",
        required_paths=[
            "learn.azure.azure-fundamentals",
            "learn.azure.data-engineer-associate",
        ],
        recommended_certs=["AZ-900", "DP-203"],
        target_weeks=10,
    ),
}

def generate_onboarding_checklist(role: str, employee_name: str) -> dict:
    """Generate a structured onboarding checklist for a new hire by role."""
    plan = ONBOARDING_PLANS.get(role)
    if not plan:
        return {"error": f"No onboarding plan for role: {role}"}

    return {
        "employee": employee_name,
        "role": plan.role,
        "target_completion_weeks": plan.target_weeks,
        "learning_paths": [
            {
                "uid": uid,
                "url": f"https://learn.microsoft.com/training/paths/{uid.split('.')[-1]}",
                "status": "not_started",
            }
            for uid in plan.required_paths
        ],
        "target_certifications": plan.recommended_certs,
        "checklist": [
            "Create Microsoft Learn account at learn.microsoft.com",
            "Complete all required learning paths (see above)",
            "Schedule AZ-900 exam within first 4 weeks",
            "Complete role-specific learning path in weeks 4–8",
            f"Schedule {plan.recommended_certs[-1]} exam by week {plan.target_weeks}",
        ],
    }

checklist = generate_onboarding_checklist("cloud-engineer", "Alex Chen")
print(json.dumps(checklist, indent=2))

Applied Skills are scenario-based assessments that produce a verifiable digital credential — distinct from certifications, which require proctored exams. Each Applied Skill covers a specific job task (e.g. 'Deploy containers with Azure Kubernetes Service') and is completed in a sandboxed Azure environment with no study guide required.
Deploy and manage containers with AKS
Deploy a multi-container app and configure scaling
Secure storage for Azure Files and Blob Storage
Configure encryption, access policies, and lifecycle rules
Configure secure access with Microsoft Entra ID
Set up RBAC, conditional access, and MFA
Build a natural language processing solution
Train and deploy an Azure AI Language model
Implement CI/CD with GitHub Actions and Azure
Build a pipeline that deploys to Azure Container Apps
Applied Skills credentials are shareable via Credly and LinkedIn. They demonstrate hands-on competency independently of exam certifications — useful for portfolios and job applications.

For teams using Microsoft Learn at scale, automated progress reports help managers identify who is on track for certification and where individuals need support. The public Learn profile API returns completion data that can feed a weekly digest.

Approaching a certification exam effectively requires a structured workflow: assess your baseline, fill gaps with targeted modules, practise with assessment questions, and validate readiness before scheduling.
Download the official Skills Measured document (PDF) from the exam page on learn.microsoft.com — this is the authoritative list of what the exam covers.
Take a practice assessment on Microsoft Learn to identify weak areas. Each assessment maps to specific learning path modules.
Complete the gaps using Learn modules or documentation, focusing on hands-on sandbox exercises rather than passive reading.
Re-take the practice assessment until you score ≥ 80% consistently.
Schedule the exam via Pearson VUE (online or test center) with a 2–3 day buffer from your last study session.

## AZ-900: Azure Fundamentals — Study Checklist

### Domain 1: Cloud Concepts (25–30%)
- [ ] IaaS / PaaS / SaaS definitions and examples
- [ ] Shared responsibility model
- [ ] Public, private, hybrid cloud trade-offs
- [ ] CapEx vs. OpEx cost model

### Domain 2: Azure Architecture and Services (35–40%)
- [ ] Regions, availability zones, region pairs
- [ ] Resource groups, subscriptions, management groups
- [ ] Core compute: VMs, App Service, AKS, Container Instances, Functions
- [ ] Core storage: Blob, Disk, Files, Queue, Table
- [ ] Core networking: VNet, Load Balancer, VPN Gateway, ExpressRoute, CDN
- [ ] Core databases: SQL Database, Cosmos DB, MySQL Flexible Server
- [ ] Core identity: Entra ID, RBAC, Conditional Access, MFA

### Domain 3: Azure Management and Governance (30–35%)
- [ ] Azure Resource Manager (ARM), Bicep, Azure Portal, CLI, PowerShell
- [ ] Azure Advisor, Azure Monitor, Service Health
- [ ] Azure Policy, Blueprints, management groups
- [ ] Cost Management, pricing calculator, TCO calculator
- [ ] Support plans: Basic / Developer / Standard / ProDirect
- [ ] Compliance: Microsoft Privacy Statement, Trust Center, Compliance Manager

import requests

def fetch_practice_assessment_results(username: str, exam_code: str) -> dict:
    """
    Fetch practice assessment completion data for a specific exam from Learn profile.
    The Learn API returns assessment data as part of the profile payload.
    """
    resp = requests.get(
        f"https://learn.microsoft.com/api/profiles/{username}",
        timeout=15,
    )
    resp.raise_for_status()
    profile = resp.json()

    assessments = profile.get("assessments", [])
    exam_assessments = [
        a for a in assessments
        if exam_code.lower() in a.get("uid", "").lower()
           or exam_code.lower() in a.get("title", "").lower()
    ]

    return {
        "exam": exam_code,
        "username": username,
        "assessments_found": len(exam_assessments),
        "best_score": max((a.get("score", 0) for a in exam_assessments), default=0),
        "attempts": len(exam_assessments),
        "ready": any(a.get("score", 0) >= 80 for a in exam_assessments),
    }

# Check AZ-900 readiness
result = fetch_practice_assessment_results("your-learn-username", "AZ-900")
if result["ready"]:
    print(f"Ready to schedule AZ-900 — best score: {result['best_score']}%")
else:
    print(f"Not ready yet — best score: {result['best_score']}%, target: 80%+")

Microsoft Learn for Organisations (previously called Microsoft Learn for Business) provides additional features for teams: course assignment, completion tracking, custom learning plans, and reporting dashboards, available through the Microsoft 365 admin center or via direct agreement with Microsoft.
Included with some M365 licenses or separate agreement

For small teams (under 20 people), managing onboarding via the public profile API and manual tracking is usually sufficient. Learn for Organisations adds value at scale — when tracking certification progress across hundreds of engineers.
Common Pitfalls When Using Microsoft Learn
Treating XP and module completion as a substitute for exam readiness — modules cover concepts but the exam tests application under time pressure.
Skipping sandbox exercises in favour of passive reading — hands-on practice is what builds retention and CLI fluency.
Not downloading the Skills Measured PDF before studying — it tells you exactly which topics appear on the exam and their weightings.
Starting with Expert-level paths (AZ-305) before completing Fundamentals (AZ-900) — each tier builds on the previous one.
Using a work Entra ID account for Learn sandboxes — sandbox environments require a personal Microsoft account (Outlook, Hotmail) to avoid conflicts with organisational policies.
Confusing Applied Skills badges with certifications — Applied Skills are scenario credentials, not exam-based; they complement but do not replace certifications for most employer requirements.

Microsoft Learn provides free, structured Azure education through modules, learning paths, and sandbox environments — covering everything from AZ-900 fundamentals to role-specific associate and expert certification preparation.
Sandbox environments give hands-on Azure practice without a personal subscription or credit card.
The Microsoft Learn catalog API (learn.microsoft.com/api/catalog/) lets you programmatically discover and assign learning paths for team onboarding.
Certifications follow a three-tier structure: Fundamentals (AZ-900), Associate (AZ-104, AZ-204, DP-203), and Expert (AZ-305, AZ-400).
Applied Skills provide verifiable scenario-based credentials for specific job tasks — completed in a sandbox environment, no proctored exam required.
Build team onboarding plans by mapping roles to required learning paths and certification targets, then track completion via the public Learn profile API.
